learning_outcomes.description: explain the importance of homeostasis in human survival, including the importance of the cellular environment and the cell membrane, cellular communication, and integration of cellular responses
describe the structure of the cells, tissues and organs that constitute some of the human organ systems

identify the principal components, relevant anatomical features and relationships of some of the human organ systems on human cadaveric specimens and communicate effectively using correct anatomical terminology
explain how human body systems maintain health
explain the integration and control of the major body systems

recognise the link between human biology and disease processes and predict some functional deficits that may occur as a result of altered structure, within an ethical framework of respect for both living and deceased humans
identify the causes, risk factors, symptoms and treatment options of common health disorders
explain the mechanisms of actions of common drugs which affect the cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, renal, respiratory, nervous, immune and endocrine systems

assessments.task_description: Your CC will discuss the frequency and style of these assessments in first week (teaching week 1) of the course during the course introduction lecture so please attend and ask questions. Further assessment details will be provided on Blackboard during the semester.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
The in-semester examination is your first major examination for this course. This in-semester examination will test the knowledge from all weeks except week 5. This examination will be MCQs only. It will comprise of 40 questions with 10 minutes of perusal time.

The exam will be a closed book invigilated exam held on campus, undertaken via the Inspera eAssessment platform. Students will be required to bring a laptop to the exam that meets the device requirements for Inspera. It is important that you check before the exam that your laptop meets the device requirements for using the Inspera Assessment platform. If you do not own a suitable laptop, you can borrow one from the Library. The Library website Get familiar with Inspera provides the latest information for students about using Inspera. Further information about the exam will be provided on Blackboard.

Have your UQ student ID card available for all your exams. 

This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of Artificial Intelligence (AI). Students are advised that the use of AI technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.assessment_title: Invigilated End of Semester Examination- VIVA
assessments.task_description:
Purpose

The VIVA examination assesses students’ ability to articulate, justify, and critically reflect on key biomedical concepts, experimental approaches, and data interpretation skills developed throughout BIOM7121. This oral examination evaluates understanding, clinical reasoning, and scientific communication in real-time. You will get plenty of practice to increase chances of success in the VIVAs.

Format

  • The examination will be conducted in person under invigilated conditions.
  • Each student will have 8 minutes total: 6 minutes for oral examination and 2 minutes for examiner deliberation. This is proposed time and might change. If it does you will be informed.
  • Examiners will consist of two academic staff members, one of whom is the course convenor or nominated assessor.
  • Sessions will be scheduled individually across designated examination days. Students will be notified of their specific time and venue at least one week in advance.


Content

Questions will draw from:

  • Lecture content, practical sessions, and prescribed readings
  • Interpretation of results and problem-solving scenarios
  • Application of knowledge to clinical or professional contexts


Preparation

Students are expected to:

  • Revise all core course materials. Be in touch with your course coordinator regularly so you can be assisted to get the best outcome
  • Be prepared to explain biomedical concepts you would have learned in lectures and practicals
  • Bring only a pen and un-annotated water bottle; no notes, devices, or additional materials are permitted


Assessment Criteria

Responses will be assessed against:

  • Accuracy and depth of biomedical knowledge
  • Clarity and structure of oral communication
  • Ability to think critically
  • Professional conduct and composure under examination conditions


Weighting

The VIVA examination is worth 15% of the final grade for BIOM7121.

Special Consideration

Students requiring adjustments due to disability, illness, or other circumstances must submit a formal request via the University’s Special Consideration prior to the scheduled examination.


This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
